A 'Limited English speaking household' is one in which no member 14 years old and over (1) speaks only English at home or (2) speaks a language other than English at home and speaks English 'Very well.'
A Central Index Key (CIK) is a unique 10-digit number assigned by the United States Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) to individuals, companies, filing agents, or foreign governments.
A Consumer Price Index measures changes in the price level of a weighted average market basket of consumer goods and services purchased by households.
A P-code, short for place code, is a kind of geocode from UN OCHA used mostly by emergency response teams.

A Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) is a greenhouse gas concentration (not emissions) trajectory adopted by the IPCC. The pathways describe different climate futures, all of which are considered possible depending on the volume of greenhouse gases (GHG) emitted in the years to come.
A Title I eligible school is a school designated under appropriate state and federal regulations as being high poverty and eligible for participation in programs authorized by Title I.

A Treasury Bill is a zero-coupon Treasury security that matures in one year or less. Treasury Bills are bought at a discount of the par value and eventually redeemed at that par value to create a positive yield to maturity.
A Treasury Bond is a Treasury security with a maturity of thirty years, and a coupon payment every six months.
A Treasury Note is a Treasury security with a maturity of 2, 3, 5, 7, or 10 years, and a coupon payment every six months.
A biological preparation that provides active acquired immunity to a particular infectious disease
A brief comment, explanation, or record.
A category representing any Advanced Placement (AP) course or subject not explicitly listed elsewhere.
A chemical element is a pure substance consisting only of atoms that all have the same numbers of protons in their nuclei and cannot be broken down into simpler substances by any chemical reaction.
A chemical substance is a form of matter having constant chemical composition and characteristic properties.
A civil union is a legally recognized arrangement similar to marriage, created primarily as a means to provide recognition in law for same-sex couples.
A claim to continue receiving unemployment benefits.
A classification of workers based on how much they have worked in the one year preceding the date of enumeration.
A climate model for projecting/forecasting measures like temperature, humidity etc.
A cold (temperature) event happens when the minimum temperature for a period (e.g. over a day) is below the reference (e.g. a long term average for the month) by a certain amount.
A collection of solar photovoltaic panels
A combined performance category representing students who either met or exceeded the achievement standard (Proficient or Advanced).
A compact and densely built area with continuous development.

A consumer is a person who intends to order or use purchased goods, products, or services primarily for personal, social, family, household and similar needs, not directly related to entrepreneurial or business activities.
A continuous disturbance of the atmosphere marked by wind and usually by rain, snow, hail, sleet, or thunder and lightning.
A criminal incident is defined as one or more offenses committed by the same offender, or group of offenders acting in concert, at the same time and place.
A cyclone is a type of storm with a large scale air mass that rotates around a strong center of low atmospheric pressure.
A cyclone of any intensity for which the primary energy source is baro clinic, that is, results from the temperature contrast between warm and cold air masses.
A dense urban area extending from the boundary of the inner urban area to the outer edge of the continuous built area.

A designated place for the purpose of sorting mail. Usually identified by a series of letters and/or digits included in a postal address. Also known locally in various English-speaking countries throughout the world as a postcode, post code, PIN, or ZIP Code.
A digestive process by which carbohydrates are broken down by microorganisms into simple molecules for absorption into the bloodstream of an animal.

A discrete tropical weather system of apparently organized convection -- generally 100 to 300 nmi in diameter -- originating in the tropics or subtropics, having a non frontal migratory character, and maintaining its identity for 24 hours or more. It may or may not be associated with a detectable perturbation of the wind field.
A facility (organization, power plant, etc.) reporting data to US EPA.

A family household is a household maintained by a householder who is in a family, and includes any unrelated people (unrelated subfamily members and/or secondary individuals) who may be residing there. The number of family households is equal to the number of families.
A family household without a house.
A firm is typically an organizational entity, comparable to the familiar use of the word company. A firm can exist in one location or have many locations and can have multiple establishments.
A formal group decision-making process by which a population chooses an individual to hold public office.
A four-year study educational institution per Carnegie classification.
A fundamental atmospheric quantity caused by air moving from high to low pressure, usually due to changes in temperature.
A general category for school subjects that cover mathematical concepts beyond the standard curriculum, often including topics like calculus, statistics, or discrete mathematics.

A general election is a political voting election where generally all or most members of a given political body are chosen. These are usually held for a nation, state, or territory's primary legislative body, and are different from by-elections (only one electorate goes to election).
A geographic area that is located outside towns and cities, typically with low population density and small settlements.
A geographic area with population density lower than urban and more than rural.
A geographic identifier that uniquely identifies all administrative/legal and statistical geographic areas for which the U.S. Census Bureau tabulates data.
A geographically localized community within a larger city, town or suburb.

A government is the system or group of people governing an organized community, often a state, but also other entities, for example, a company. While all types of organizations have governance, the word government is often used more specifically to refer to the approximately 200 independent national governments on Earth, as well as their subsidiary organizations.
A grid on the Earth's surface approximately corresponding to 0.25 degree resolution for attaching climate related observations. These are defined within the context of a specific country.
A grid on the Earth's surface approximately corresponding to 0.50 degree resolution for attaching climate related observations. These are defined within the context of a specific country.
A group of chemical elements that react with metals to produce a wide range of salts.
A group of serotypes that have one or more antigens in common

A group of students who work through a curriculum together to achieve the same academic degree together. The cohort membership gives the details of month of joining and number of years to achieve graduation.

A grouping of statistical variables that have similar characteristics, for the purposes of answering natural-language queries. An example could be a group of variables that are all related to the number of people in a particular place, but have different measured properties.

A hate crime is a criminal offense against a person or property motivated in whole or in part by an offender's bias against a race, religion, disability, sexual orientation, ethnicity, gender, or gender identity.
A heat (temperature) event happens when the maximum temperature for a period (e.g. over a day) exceeds the reference (e.g. a long term average for the month) by a certain amount.
A high-school level mathematics course that continues the study of algebraic concepts, including complex numbers, polynomials, and logarithms.

A married couple is a husband and wife enumerated as members of the same household. The married couple may or may not have children living with them. A married couple household is one where the household is maintained by a husband and wife.
A material that, when freshly prepared, polished, or fractured, shows a lustrous appearance, and conducts electricity and heat relatively well.
A measure of acres reported for a fire.  More specifically, the number of acres within the current perimeter of a specific, individual incident, including unburned and unburnable islands.

A unique identifier for a data import, containing a set of structured records obtained from a data source. A Provenance may represent a subset of records contained in a (large) Dataset, or may represent a single Dataset. For example, 'dc/base/Census ACS5 Year Survey_Subject Tables_S1502' is one Provenance of the Dataset 'dc/d/United States Census Bureau_American Community Survey Acs' provided by the US Census Bureau, and represents one table of a database.

A value of the stat Type property of a Statistical Variable. Indicates that the variable reports the skewness over a range of observations. An example is the skewness value of a range of temperature measurements.

A value of the stat Type property of a Statistical Variable. Indicates that the variable reports the standard deviation over a range of observations. An example is the standard deviation from the average test score of a population.

School Type is a classification of schools conducting elementary and/or secondary instruction programs according to the ordinary or special instructional needs of students. Four categories of schools are: Regular School: A public elementary/secondary school that does not focus primarily on vocational, special, or alternative education. Special Education School: Public elementary/secondary school that focuses primarily on education of one or more of the following types of students: hard of hearing, deaf, speech-impaired, health-impaired, orthopedically impaired, intellectual disability, seriously emotionally disturbed, multi-handicapped, visually handicapped, or deaf and blind, and adapts curriculum, materials or instructions for students served. Vocational School: Public elementary/secondary school that focuses primarily on vocational education, and provides education in one or more semi-skilled technical operations. Other/Alternative School: A public elementary/secondary school that addresses the needs of students which typically cannot be met in a regular school, provides nontraditional education, serves as an adjunct to a regular school, and falls outside of the categories of regular, special education, or vocational education.

Superfund sites are polluted locations in the United States requiring a long-term response to clean up hazardous material contaminations.

Superfund sites can be categorized into two classes (i) In the National Priority List (NPL) site and (ii) Non-NPL site. The other means through which clean-up operations at sites are financed include (i)individuals or companies responsible for the problems can clean up voluntarily with EPA or State Supervision. (ii) The responsible party or parties can be ordered to clean up by Federal or State legal action. (iii)A State or local government can choose to assume the responsibility to clean up without Federal dollars. (iv)The Superfund Trust Fund can pay for the cleanup, then seek to recover the costs later from the responsible party or parties. A site needs to be on the NPL in order for it to be eligible to financed through the Superfund Trust Fund.
Superfund sites have sampling sites from where the level of contamination is measured over time. Some examples of sampling sites are wells, monitoring stations.

This variable indicates whether a school participates in the National School Lunch Program, and, if so, under what special provisions. The Healthy, Hunger-Free Kids Act of 2010 (PL 111-296) includes provisions for determining free and reduced-price lunch (FRL) eligibility that may affect the reliability and availability of the FRL counts reported to EDFacts (FS033). Under provisions 2 and 3 of the law, annual certification of individual students is not required. A new provision, the Community Eligibility Option (CEO or Provision 4), eliminates the requirement for individual eligibility information once a school has determined a baseline percentage of FRL eligible students. These changes may result in missing or out of date FRL counts. Education researchers frequently use FRL eligibility as an indicator of student socioeconomic status (SES). The NSLP status provides these researchers with an indication of the reliability of the FRL counts reported in this file.
